LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT IS DENIED. THERE IS A TRIABLE DISPUTE WHETHER THE CONDUCT OF THE BUS OPERATOR, FOR WHICH THE CITY IS VICARIOUSLY LIABLE, FELL BELOW THE HEIGHTENED STANDARD OF CARE APPLICABLE TO COMMON CARRIERS PER CCP 2100. A REASONABLE JUROR COULD FIND THAT THE DRIVER FAILED TO "TO DO ALL THAT HUMAN CARE, VIGILANCE, AND FORESIGHT REASONABLY CAN DO UNDER THE CIRCUMSTANCES" (LOPEZ V. SOUTHERN CAL. RAPID TRANSIT DIST. (1985) 40 CAL.3D 780, 785) BASED ON EVIDENCE THAT MR. CELESTE DID NOT SEE MR. SCHENK NOR THE OTHER PEOPLE HOLDING THE BAR AND DID NOT WARN MR. SHENK USING THE AP SYSTEM TO MOVE HIS HAND AWAY FROM THE BAR DESPITE HIS TRAINING TO USE HIS MIRRORS AND NOTHING WAS BLOCKING HIS VIEW IN THE MIRRORS. THERE IS EVIDENCE THAT MR. CELESTE WAS TRAINED TO USE HIS MIRRORS TO CHECK THE INTERIOR OF THE BUS BEFORE OPENING THE DOOR, NOT TO OPEN THE DOOR IF HE SEES A PASSENGER BLOCKING THE DOOR, AND TO ALERT A PASSENGER TO MOVE HIS LIMBS AWAY FROM THE DOOR BEFORE OPENING THE DOOR. BECAUSE MR. SCHENK DEFEATS THIS MOTION BASED ON A THEORY THAT THE CITY IS VICARIOUSLY LIABLE FOR THE CONDUCT OF THE BUS OPERATOR, THERE IS NO NEED TO ADDRESS THE ISSUE OF THE VIABILITY OF ANY DANGEROUS CONDITION THEORY ASSERTED BY MR. SCHENK. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN; CLERK: CYNTHIA HERBERT; REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR# 8600 =302/HK

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant City And County Of San Franciscos Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment
Off Calendar Aug-13-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Atty. Michael Schreiber of Bracamontes & Vlasak, Tel. No. 1-415-835-6777
appearing for the Plaintiff;
Atty. Amy D, Super, Deputy City Attorney, Tel. No. 1-415-554-3831 appearing
for the Defendant.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Defendant City and County of San Francisco's motion for summary judgment is denied. There is a triable dispute whether the conduct of the bus operator, for which the City is vicariously liable, fell below the heightened standard of care applicable to common carriers per CCP 2100. A reasonable juror could find that the driver failed to "to do all that human care, vigilance, and foresight reasonably can do under the circumstances" (Lopez v. Southern Cal. Rapid Transit Dist. (1985) 40 Cal.3d 780, 785) based on evidence that Mr. Celeste did not see Mr. Schenk nor the other people holding the bar and did not warn Mr. Shenk using the AP system to move his hand away from the bar despite his training to use his mirrors and nothing was blocking his view in the mirrors. There is evidence that Mr. Celeste was trained to use his mirrors to check the interior of the bus before opening the door, not to open the door if he sees a passenger blocking the door, and to alert a passenger to move his limbs away from the door before opening the door. Because Mr. Schenk defeats this motion based on a theory that the City is vicariously liable for the conduct of the bus operator, there is no need to address the issue of the viability of any dangerous condition theory asserted by Mr. Schenk. Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR# 8600 =302/hk
